sentences.text: Gleiwitz, and--
Yeah, we came to a forest.
And suddenly, there was a big square in front of us.
The SS with the machines were running to be ahead of us, and they start shooting at us.
They put the machine guns down and start shooting in the people, to kill.
For no reason.
Walter and me, we ran away, because we were right by the forest.
After the square was the woods.
We ran away, Walter and me, we ran in the forest.
And we met there an elderly guy that ran away from Yugoslavia.
And he says, children, from where are you?
So we said, we are from Austria, Vienna.
He says, you are from Vienna?
You know what?
We'll walk to Vienna.
And if we had stopped, if we had stayed there, we would be freed two days later from the Russians.
So we start walking.
We came to a street.
Oops, the SS captured us again.
Again to march.
We are marched all the way to-- to Tresen.
Walter and me, we ran away in Tresen.
On the road, many times, when I wanted to sit down, Walter grabbed me and picked me up.
When Walter want to sit down, I grabbed him.
Because whoever was sitting down, couldn't walk, they shot him.
During the march-- even before-- whoever couldn't walk, they shot him and threw him in the ditch on the side of the road.
In Tresen, Walter and me, we ran away.
We were swollen.
Everything was infected, our bodies, from the-- from laying in the-- in the beds, on the wood and all that.
We went into a house.
It was a house from, I don't know.
In Tresen, Germans, they asked us, what you doing here?
We were dirty, and hungry, and Muselmann.
We ran away from the Russians, we are Hitlerjugend.
They let us sleep there.
In the morning, they said, you have to go.
They gave us some food, we had to go.
We went outside.
In Tresen, in the street, they had in-- they had walls on the street with openings.
And Walter and me went in to take out this-- the-- the mark from concentration camp.
And the moment we took this out, they captured us again.
And this was SS and-- and-- and Volksdeutsche, Germans that they ran away from the Russian.
They took us kids.
They saw our shoes, they took off our shoes and put those wooden pantoffels on, wood.
And we had to carry their suitcases.
They ran away from the Russian.
We had to carry.
And we worked with them about a week.
Altogether, the time was about three weeks.
A lot of people from this way were killed, whoever couldn't go.
Only Walter and me, we-- we really, one saved the other's life.
They brought us to a camp that was called Gross-Rosen, and there we got a little bit soup.
It was dirty.
Mud.
It was unbelievable.
We stood there for a week, and they put us then on feed wagons, on-- on trains.
Open-- open train wagons, railroad wagons, open.
Probably between 150 and 180 men in one wagon.
We had-- we were staying in this wagon like sardines, and we were on the train for one week without food.
The only thing we had was warm urinate and snow.
We had nothing to eat, nothing to drink.
After this week, we came on in a place that was called Weimar.
Again, we had to go out of the trains.
Everything with sticks, everything with dogs.
Screaming, yelling, rouse.
And we walked up of a-- up a mountain to a concentration camp called Buchenwald.
When we arrived in Buchenwald, we had to undress.
First of all, I forgot, all the time in Birkenau, we were full with lice.
Whenever we had time, we were standing and killing the lice.
But when we came to Buchenwald, we had to take off all our clothes.
We stood the whole night outside for the disinfection, naked.
In Buchenwald, in January 1945, in snow, in cold weather, a whole night naked.
Till they put-- took us in in a room, they put us in Lysol water, shaved us, the whole body.
Shaved us, and we got clothes, and we got a piece of bread.
And we got a piece of metal for next day's soup.
Walter and me, we-- they took us in, and we went in a block.
Walter and me, one piece of bread we ate.
And one piece of bread, I said, Walter, this we'll-- we'll save for tomorrow.
And I put it here under my arm.
And we came in a block.
And in the moment we came into the block, Ukraines and Polacks-- I don't know from where they came-- they touched me all over, they found my piece of bread and took it.
And I cried.
That's-- that's one thing, then I start crying for this piece of bread.
Walter, I said, they took this piece of bread.
We'll have nothing to eat tomorrow.
They put us in in Block 66 in Buchenwald.
And before Block 66, I think we were in another block, because Block 66 was a children's block.
We were laying there on bunks three stories high, seven or eight people.
When we wanted to turn, we have to talk together that we'll turn.
All-- all-- the whole bed-- all together, we had to turn.
People urinated from the other floor, from the second bunk or the third.
We had-- we were laying on the beds on wood, nothing underneath.
It was terrible.
At night, people came in and stole this piece of metal, or killed people to get this piece of metal for the next day's soup.
Then they put us in Block 66, where we were all children.
Block 66 in Buchenwald was block-- children's block.
Again, we have to-- had to start working.
We-- we start working.
They took us out of the camp to mountains, where we had to load rocks on a-- was a little railing with--
Wagons, little wagons that we had to put stones in it.
But I remember during the march, when we all marched in Buchenwald to work, we had to sing.
And when-- we had to sing a song that goes something like this.
[SINGING IN GERMAN]
We had-- we had to-- when we marched, we had to sing it.
In the camp, the kapo that was in our block.
Before we had to march, he teached us.
He said, you have to-- when you march, you have to sing.
The song was like, the-- the heaven is red, and the-- and the-- the forest is black.
[SINGING SOFTLY] And-- and-- [SINGING SOFTLY] And we are carrying our baggage in the back, a little bit bread.
In our heart, we have-- we are worried about it.
And we know how beautiful the-- it is in the freedom.
But Buchenwald, we don't cry, and we don't scream, because we know that one day will come, and we will be free.
That was the song that we had to sing.
Yeah, but we were-- we-- we knew we will never get out of there.
We-- then we had to work in this stone thing.
It-- in Buchenwald, Walter and me and the other kids who were there, we worked over in this-- in this stone mountain.
It was very hard.
During the time they told us things.
Yeah, in-- in Buchenwald, they brought-- this I know and we saw-- there was barracks.
There was Russian soldiers they-- they captured.
And there was one barrack with a weight, and the Russian soldiers came in for a check-up, and behind the weight, the SS was standing, and everybody was shot.
They had ditches for the blood.
And all the Russian captures, they got killed there.
They-- they made appells for the Russians to stay outside in an appell.
The head of the SS, the woman, went through selection, selected all the Russians that had beautiful [GERMAN].
How do you call it?
Tattoos.
Tattoos.
They had beautiful tattoos on their body.
And they took them, they cut it out, and they made lamps, handbags, all kind of things that she liked.
And they-- they-- they-- it still exists till today.
We were there in-- in-- in-- in Buchenwald till it came-- before April.
I remember Walter's birthday.
It was on 4th of April.
But before that, they sent all kind of transports out of the camps.
We were told a lot of thousands of people they took outside the camps and killed them.
Put in barracks and put bombs in it, shooting, all kind of killings.
They want to get rid of the people.
I remember Walter's birthday was on 4th of April, that we saved up bread crumbs.
And on his birthday, 4th of April, 1945, we celebrated his birthday.
We took the bread crumbs and cooked them.
And we had to eat a lot.
This was our-- a great birthday.
And on 5th, 6th of April, suddenly, we heard cannons.
They told us the Americans are coming closer.
We had a savings, Walter and me, sardine cans, half with margarine, half with jam.
But we still didn't eat it on his birthday, because this was our savings, our life savings.
We, heard it, and suddenly, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] around the 10th, suddenly the SS start to run away.
The camp was free.
I mean, we were alone.
We didn't know what happened.
And suddenly, the American army came in.
The day that the camp was free, the kids-- what I forgot to say is that before the-- the Americans came, they came to our block.
Our blockalteste was a Czech, Tony.
And he told the SS, there is no Jewish kids here.
The next day, they found out that there are, yes, Jewish.
Tony, the Czech, the blockalteste, told us, kids run away.
Because if you don't run away, they will kill you.
They will collect you.
So we run in the sewer pipes.
We run-- we knew already that the Americans coming, and they want to get us.
All the kids, we were running away.
We were running in sewer lines, different places, I don't remember.
When the-- when the Germans left, we were all so hungry, the only thing we went-- we went to the-- where the Germans kept the dogs.
We took dog food.
We filled our shirts.
We were sitting-- when the American army came in to us.
We were sitting and eating dog food and just cried, please, take us away, they'll come back and get us.
Take us away, the Germans will come and kill us.
Take us.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When the Americans came, it was a big day.
And they took the children, and they put us out in the SS blocks.
They brought-- the first couple days, they brought a lot of food in the camps.
People started eating, and they died.
We all said they died like flea, like the flies.
Because they were-- they brought soups made with pig.
Fat, heavy fat, and this.
People, they didn't eat for years and months.
When they start eating, they get diarrhea, they died.
And to us, they gave us ration from the American army.
Chocolate, the first couple days.
Then we went down to the villages, to the German villages.
We took chicken and eggs and-- and food, and we cooked.
We had a room of only Viennese food.
What I want to mention then, they didn't give us no nurse.
We were all alone.
Nobody cared about us.
And one of our kids, a Viennese boy-- his name was Hans Gerson.
I later on found his aunt in Vienna.
He found something.
And we went always to the camps to see how the people are.
From the SS blocks it was probably a 10 to 15 minutes walk.
When we came back, we found only the room was pieces of meat.
We stood in Buchenwald from January till April 11, 1945, we were freed.
And then I stood-- I don't remember, a month or two months.
I know they-- I found a picture that I found myself, that May 9, they took a picture they made in Buchenwald.
A gathering with the rabbi from the American army.
And I found myself in the picture.
Otherwise I wouldn't know that I was still there.
Walter and me, we went back to Salzburg, Austria, and we decided that he will go to Israel, and I will go back to the ghetto to find some families.
And really, I went back.
I found two aunts.
Two elderly aunts, two sisters of my father.
I took them-- how we went, I took them, I went, we came to Vienna.
I was sick.
My body, my fro-- my feet was frozen.
My sides, my bones, all was infected.
And we found my cousin, the son of my aunt.
And he was married then.
I have to mention her name.
Her name was Mitzi, Mitzi Boch.
His wife, she saved his life.
Because when Eichmann wanted to send him to the camp, to the gas, from Vienna, she hided him in Vienna.
She was not Jewish.
But she really saved his life.
And she saw me, she took me to her place.
She cleaned me, she washed me, she fed me.
And after three, four weeks or month, a month and a half, I start working in the Jewish community in Vienna, to write down names from people that came back from the camps.
And after a week or so came in a soldier, dressed.
He had here a-- he was from the British army, in the Jewish regiment, Palestine regiment.
And he came in and asked about if there's somebody written there, a Riegler.
Yes.
So what's-- what-- I says, listen.
My name-- my mother's name is Riegler.
I'm a Riegler.
We start talking.
We found out he was my cousin, that he left before the war to Israel-- to Palestine, then.
When he heard that I'm a cousin, he came about a group of 10, 15 soldiers from Italy.
He was stationed in Mestre, near Venice.
He says, you come with me.
I didn't want to stay in Austria for no money in the world.
My cousin says, you stay with me, I give you everything you want.
I said, I don't stay in Austria if you give me all Austria.
I cannot stay in a place-- what they did to me, to my family, to my-- to all my relatives.
So he put me on-- soldiers, British soldiers, and we went-- not legally-- we went through the Austrian-Italian border.
And he brought me to Mestre, to his camp, where he fed me.
And there I saw grapes and watermelon and fruit.
And he took me to Venice, and I had their food.
I was-- it was something, I-- I didn't believe in it.
But then he couldn't take me to Palestine.
He-- he sent me to a camp of children.
They were-- they collected-- they were collecting in Italy, near Milano in Selvino-- it was the home for the Hitlerjugend from Mussolini.
They collected children.
And he sent me over there.
And from there I was a future to go wherever I want.
We came to this home, and there we had already nurses and teachers from Palestine, they came.
And there was one [INAUDIBLE] Mendel-- we called him Uncle Mendel-- that he, from the British army, brought us food.
Every day came the truck with Uncle Mendel that brought food to the children.
And there we had teachers, and there were children, all ages.
And one of the-- two, three weeks later came a group from Magenta, other children.
And who was between the children?
Walter.
And we met again.
Instead, he should be in Israel, we met in Italy.
So we found out we had an aunt in Italy-- in London.
We wrote to her, and she got a special visa because our parents were gassed in-- in the camps.
She got a visa from the Queen that we could go to England.
And when we got the visa, I went to my teacher from Israel.
I says, listen.
Or you send me to Israel, or I go to London.
I go to England, Palestine.
And he saw that I could go, he says no, no.
You go to Palestine.
Walter was sick.
He couldn't go to Palestine.
I went with a ship, [INAUDIBLE], it's called. 900 people on one boat.
We came to-- to-- on the-- on the front of Haifa and this.
A British army ship captured us and took us in a camp in-- in Palestine, in Atlit.
So here I came from Italy to freedom.
Again, the British put me in a-- in a camp.
But we were not-- but we got food, everything.
They released us after three weeks.
And after these three weeks, the whole-- all the group from Selvino, all the children, we came Hanita.
To Kibbutz Hanita.
We were the first-- they called it the first Aliyat Hanoar, the first group of children that the kibbutz in-- in Palestine got there.
So we came there, the first group.
In-- in Kibbutz Hanita, we had-- we start studying a half day, and a half day we worked.
And I was there in Kibbutz Hanita [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] oh, January, February, '45, a couple months.
Then I found out that my uncle, that left before-- Wait, now, January '45, not '46?
'46, Yeah.
In January '46, I found out I have an uncle, that he left in 1940 from Vienna.
That he had to leave in 24 hours.
I found out my aunt died on the boat.
I found him and a cousin.
And I left the kibbutz.
I went to Haifa.
Bat Galim, it's called, a little village.
And in 1947, I went on my-- I went to the Israeli-- to the-- still the British, but I went to the Israeli army.
Free-- on my free own will, I didn't have to go.
And in Bat Galim, I met friends and girls--
I met my wife right then in '48. '47, '48.
One of my friends that lived in the same city-- I didn't have nobody-- when I was in the army, I-- I-- his parents adopted me like a son.
I ate there, I slept with him.
And there was a group of people, boys and girls.
And between his sister's friend was my wife, Menora, that I met then.
My wife was an Israeli-born sabra.
I was three years in the Israeli army, and I was freed in 1950.
In 1950, I came to my parents-- my wife's parents.
And that time my wife became an Israeli soldier too for five, six months.
And then was talking that if you get married, the girls, they let them out from the army.
So I came to her parents, I says, listen, I want to get married.
I didn't have nothing.
I didn't have nothing, really.
So they said, why you want to marry now?
Marry later, and this.
But, in the end, they-- we got married in January-- no, no, no, not January.
December 1950. 26th of the December, 1950, we married.
Her mother, a [YIDDISH], I don't know in English means--
An angel.
Her father, all her family.
She had two sisters, one sister was married.
They took me in, in their home.
We were married.
Her mother made ready the wedding.
Everything, the cakes cooks, everything.
We were married, my wife and me.
We had five Israeli pounds.
On our honeymoon we went to Tel Aviv, and another cousin from Rehovot Hobart gave us 10 pounds.
And that's all what we started.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She got married, and she left the army, and I started working.
After the army I learned to be a plumber, welding.
And after that, I became-- went on my own.
A year later, I became a contractor, plumbing contractor, welding contractor.
I did new-- new kibbutzim, new villages.
I put the pipes in.
I worked on the Syrian border.
Then I worked with walkie-talkies on the excavator.
Now you can work, now you have to go back.
In the morning, we found bombs in our pipes.
It was not easy.
In 1952, my first son was born.
His name, we gave him the name of her-- her father, my father, Adi Samuel.
And but it was very hard.
I worked outside the cities in new villages, new kibbutzim.
I came home once a week and once in two weeks.
And--
My first son-- when I worked in the village near Tiberius, I had a dream one day-- it was Thursday before we went home-- that her mother sits next to us and tells us kids, don't cry.
And then when I came home, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one of my-- I went through the city-- through the street where my brother-in-law lives.
My wife was standing with friends.
And they ran down, and they told me my son died.
He was six months old.
He-- middle of the night, he couldn't breathe.
There's a sickness like that, then we didn't know.
After all this what I went through, my first son.
It was very hard for us.
And later on, we had another, my oldest son, born Ron, in '54.
In '56, my second son, Tom.
And during that time, Walter came to visit.
Walter went to England, left England and went to United States.
He visited us from England, then he went to the United States.
He came to visit us.
After I had my two kids, one day, he came and became a contractor.
Walter came, took out papers from the consul of United States that we can emigrate to the United States.
I says, no.
He took out the papers.
A year later, we got papers, if we want, we can go to the United States.
In 1962, we decided to come here for a year to see what's happen.
And since then, we're in United States.
We came here from New York.
He took us by car here.
We came to Los Angeles.
I start-- the first two months, I was-- it was very disappointing.
I couldn't find a-- a job, even to clean streets.
I was ready for 50 cents.
But-- and I was ready already to go back, and one day called a Hungarian guy, Steve.
He says, Ernie, tomorrow we'll check you out.
Come to work for us in a factory that makes pita, all kind of Mexican tortillas.
I came there, they checked me out as a welder, he says, yeah, we will take you.
You are Jewish, we will pay you all the holidays.
You get $2.40 an hour.
And I was very happy.
I worked there for three months, but I wanted to go in in plumbing.
I wanted to go in-- as a welder and pipefitter, but I couldn't get in because I was Jewish.
I found out, the Jews, you could-- you can't-- it's very hard to get in the pipefitters.
There was friends, like I met [INAUDIBLE] Golditch.
He wanted to help me, wanted to take a lawyer.
I said thank you, forget about it.
But I went in in plumbing.
I started working for a plumbing company here in United States for two years.
And I decided to go on my own.
During that time, it was very hard.
I worked, I had to learn English.
I came here, I didn't know English.
I studied English.
I studied plumbing.
My wife helped me a lot.
She knew English from Israel.
She was always on my side.
From the day we married, she always was on my side.
She went through thick and thin.
All my life, she went through and helped me.
Huh?
Now I'm retired.
I went on my own.
I-- for many years, then I decided to leave and go back to Israel.
In 1970, I sold my business.
I sold my house here.
I went back, but it was too hard for me.
I decided to come back for-- to work again and get my retirement from the union.
And then I start working for Liberty Plumbing here, for many years.
I had a boss-- his name is Milt Levitt-- that he was like a father to me.
Till today we are friends.
And about 10 years ago, during that time, I liked apartment buildings and land.
And every penny that I could save, I bought buildings.
I managed it, I did the work, everything.
And about 10 years ago, I retired.
And one of my sons-- the middle one, Tom-- got married.
And I waited very long for that.
I waited for very long.
He married a beautiful girl, Terri Sklar.
They are here.
You will see.
And this was one of my happiest days in my life.
And I have two grandchildren.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Hanley, Hanley Esther, and the son is Andrew, and then Leo.
They gave him my brother's name.
And my father's name.
Because her grandfather was Samuel, too.
So his name is Andrew Leo Samuel.
So I brought back my brother and the name of my father.
During that time, lately, I-- it was very important for me to do something for the camps.
And I was connected with the Israeli Coin Corporation, that they made a coin of the concentration camp.
I have here the camp-- the coin they made in the memory of the six million.
And they made in Los Angeles, in the park, a memorial.
A big plaque for my camp, the B lager, famililager, and the boys of Birkenau.
That this relieved me a little bit.
The testimony I do because all-- all the years, I never talked.
Not to my children.
I never talked.
I-- I never was sitting with them together and told them my story.
Because it's very hard to believe.
It's very hard to live with it.
Because I live with it every day of my life.
I think about my-- my family.
My brother, my mother.
There's not a day that I don't remember.
It's maybe hard to believe.
I never talked about till some years ago.
But it's important.
I want to leave it so my children and other people should know what was really true.
Because there's people today, they say that it-- it didn't happen, it's all a lie.
So I think this is a proof that it is not a lie.
That we are still-- everything is true, and that we are still here.
And I want to leave it for my grandchildren, for my sons, to know what their grandpa--
